---
title: "07 · Fractions"
subtitle: "When the leftover gets cut up — a new kind of number"
lesson-id: "t1.fractions-as-parts"
tier: 1
prerequisites: ["t1.divide-fair-share"]
unlocks: ["t1.decimals", "t1.ratio-proportion"]
applied-domains: ["databases", "ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](../06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)?
2. Can you compute $12 \div 4 = 3$ and $13 \div 4 = 3$ remainder 1?
3. Have you ever cut a cake, a pizza, or a chapati into equal pieces?
:::

## Touch

Drag to choose **how many equal pieces** to cut a circle into, and **how
many** of those pieces to take.

```{ojs}
viewof denom = Inputs.range([1, 12], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "Cut into:"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof numer = Inputs.range([0, 12], {value: 1, step: 1, label: "Take:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 360, H = 360, cx = W/2, cy = H/2, r = 130;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:360px; display:block; margin: 0 auto;");
  const taken = Math.min(numer, denom);
  for (let i = 0; i < denom; i++) {
    const a0 = -Math.PI/2 + 2 * Math.PI * i / denom;
    const a1 = -Math.PI/2 + 2 * Math.PI * (i + 1) / denom;
    const x0 = cx + r * Math.cos(a0), y0 = cy + r * Math.sin(a0);
    const x1 = cx + r * Math.cos(a1), y1 = cy + r * Math.sin(a1);
    const large = (a1 - a0) > Math.PI ? 1 : 0;
    const path = `M ${cx},${cy} L ${x0},${y0} A ${r},${r} 0 ${large} 1 ${x1},${y1} Z`;
    svg.append("path")
      .attr("d", path)
      .attr("fill", i < taken ? "#90caf9" : "#f5f5f5")
      .attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);
  }
  // Extra slice notation if numer > denom (improper)
  return svg.node();
}
```

```{ojs}
{
  const taken = Math.min(numer, denom);
  return html`<div style="text-align:center; font-size:1.5rem; margin-top:0.5rem;">
    <span style="font-family:monospace;">${taken} / ${denom}</span>
    &nbsp;=&nbsp; "${taken} of ${denom} equal pieces"
  </div>`;
}
```

The number on top — how many pieces you took — is the **numerator**.
The number on the bottom — how many pieces the whole was cut into — is
the **denominator**.

## Wonder

1. Set the cut to 4, take 4. What did you take? Set the cut to 4, take
   8. What does that even mean?
2. Set the cut to 8, take 2. Then set the cut to 4, take 1. The shaded
   region looks the same. Is there a relationship between $\tfrac{2}{8}$
   and $\tfrac{1}{4}$?
3. The number $\tfrac{1}{2}$ — where does it sit on the
   [number line](../02-number-line/index.qmd)?

## Discover

### Problem 1

You have **2 pizzas**. You want to share them between **3 friends**.
What does each friend get?

You can't give each friend a whole pizza (only 2 pizzas, 3 friends). You
can't give them whole halves either (you'd have 4 halves but 3 friends).
What's the right way to cut?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Cut each pizza into 3 equal pieces. Now you have 6 pieces total. Share
them.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Cut each pizza into 3 equal slices: that's 6 slices. Each friend gets 2
slices. Each slice is **one third** of a pizza, so each friend gets **two
thirds**, written
$$\frac{2}{3}.$$

This is the answer to "$2 \div 3$" — a number that *did not exist* in
our system before fractions. Division now always has an answer; we just
needed to invent the number to hold it.
</details>

### Problem 2

Take a pie chart cut into **8** equal pieces and shade **2** of them.
Now take a pie chart cut into **4** equal pieces and shade **1**. Is the
shaded area the same?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Yes — exactly the same.** Two-eighths and one-quarter cover the same
fraction of the whole. They are different *names* for the same number:
$$\frac{2}{8} = \frac{1}{4}.$$

Multiplying or dividing both numerator and denominator by the same
non-zero number gives an *equivalent fraction*. This is the same
"different names for the same number" idea we met for whole numbers in
[Lesson 1](../01-what-is-a-number/index.qmd).
</details>

### Problem 3

What is **$\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{4}$** as a single fraction?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Imagine cutting a circle into 4 equal pieces. Half of it is **2** of
those pieces. A quarter is **1**. So you have...
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Rewrite $\tfrac{1}{2}$ as $\tfrac{2}{4}$ (using the equivalence from
*P2*). Now we are adding fractions with the *same denominator*:
$$\frac{2}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{4}.$$

**Rule discovered:** to add fractions, first make the denominators the
same; then add only the numerators.
</details>

### Problem 4

What is **$\tfrac{3}{4}$ of $20$** sweets?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"Of" usually means *multiplication*. $\tfrac{3}{4}$ of 20 means: split 20
into 4 equal parts, take 3 of them.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Split 20 into 4 equal parts: $20 \div 4 = 5$ sweets in each part. Take 3
parts: $3 \times 5 = 15$ sweets. So $\tfrac{3}{4}$ of $20 = 15$.

This is also $\tfrac{3}{4} \times 20 = \tfrac{3 \times 20}{4} =
\tfrac{60}{4} = 15$. **A fraction times a whole number** = (numerator ×
whole) ÷ denominator.

This is also where Multiplication-as-Area gets *generalised*: 0.75 of
a strip — three-quarters of it — is $\tfrac{3}{4} \times \text{length}$.
The rectangle picture survives even when the numbers are no longer whole.
</details>

### Problem 5

Set the widget to "cut into 3, take 5". What does the picture look like?
What does the *fraction* $\tfrac{5}{3}$ mean?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You've taken more pieces than fit in one whole circle. So the picture
maxes out at "all shaded" — but the fraction $\tfrac{5}{3}$ still has a
meaning.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{5}{3}$ means **5 thirds**, which is more than one whole (since
$\tfrac{3}{3}$ is already one whole). Specifically, 5 thirds = 3 thirds
+ 2 thirds = $1 + \tfrac{2}{3} = 1\tfrac{2}{3}$.

A fraction with numerator ≥ denominator is called **improper** and
represents a number ≥ 1. We can write it either as $\tfrac{5}{3}$ or as
the **mixed number** $1\tfrac{2}{3}$ — they are the same number.

The widget had to cap at "fully shaded" because one circle holds at most
3 thirds. To show $\tfrac{5}{3}$ honestly, we'd need a second circle.
The fraction itself is undisturbed; the picture has its own limits.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The Indian astronomer-mathematician **Bhāskara II**, writing his
*Līlāvatī* in 1150 CE, gave the modern bar-form fraction $\tfrac{p}{q}$
its full algebraic life — addition, subtraction, multiplication, division
by reciprocal, all already there. *Līlāvatī* (named for his daughter)
phrases problems as little stories — bees on a lotus, peacocks on a
pillar — and was used as a textbook in India for over six hundred years.
The pizza-and-pie pictures you just used are direct descendants of his
worked examples.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A fraction isn't a "broken number" — it's the answer division gave us
when the leftovers refused to disappear. Once we accept $\tfrac{p}{q}$ as
a real number, division *always* has an answer (except by zero), and the
gaps in the number line start filling in.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **fraction** is written $\dfrac{p}{q}$ where $p$ is the **numerator**
("how many parts") and $q$ is the **denominator** ("the size of each
part: 1 of $q$ equal pieces of a whole"), with $q \neq 0$.

The fraction $\dfrac{p}{q}$ *is* the answer to $p \div q$.

Two fractions are **equivalent** if multiplying (or dividing) numerator
and denominator by the same non-zero number gives one from the other:
$$\frac{p}{q} = \frac{p \times k}{q \times k} \quad \text{for any } k \neq 0.$$

To **add or subtract** fractions, first rewrite them with a common
denominator, then add or subtract the numerators.

To **multiply** $\dfrac{a}{b} \times \dfrac{c}{d}$, multiply numerators
and denominators: $\dfrac{a \times c}{b \times d}$.

:::

> *Numerator on top — how many parts you have;*
> *Denominator below — the size of each piece.*
> *Common denominators must come first to add;*
> *Multiply across to multiply.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $\dfrac{2}{3} + \dfrac{1}{6}$.

A competent solver finds a **common denominator** first.

1. The denominators are 3 and 6. The smallest common multiple is 6.
2. Rewrite $\tfrac{2}{3}$ with denominator 6: multiply top and bottom by
   2 to get $\tfrac{4}{6}$.
3. Now both fractions share the denominator 6:
   $$\frac{4}{6} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{5}{6}.$$

**Answer:** $\dfrac{5}{6}$. Check by picture: cut a circle into 6
pieces, shade 4 (which is two-thirds) and then 1 more — five out of six.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Write three fractions equivalent to $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{2}{4}$, $\tfrac{3}{6}$, $\tfrac{50}{100}$ — multiply top and
bottom by any non-zero number.
</details>

**W2.** Compute $\dfrac{3}{8} + \dfrac{2}{8}$ and $\dfrac{7}{10} -
\dfrac{3}{10}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{5}{8}$ and $\tfrac{4}{10} = \tfrac{2}{5}$. With common
denominators, add or subtract only the numerators.
</details>

**W3.** Find $\dfrac{2}{5}$ of 35.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$35 \div 5 = 7$, then $2 \times 7 = 14$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Compute $\dfrac{1}{3} + \dfrac{1}{4}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Common denominator: smallest multiple of 3 and 4 is 12.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{1}{3} = \tfrac{4}{12}$. $\tfrac{1}{4} = \tfrac{3}{12}$. Sum:
$\tfrac{7}{12}$.
</details>

**P2.** Compute $\dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{4}{5}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For multiplication: multiply across. No common denominator needed.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{2 \times 4}{3 \times 5} = \tfrac{8}{15}$.

Picture (the rectangle returns): take a 1-by-1 square. Shade
horizontally to $\tfrac{2}{3}$ of its height, vertically to $\tfrac{4}{5}$
of its width. The doubly-shaded inner rectangle is $\tfrac{2}{3} \times
\tfrac{4}{5} = \tfrac{8}{15}$ of the whole.
</details>

**P3.** Convert the improper fraction $\dfrac{17}{5}$ to a mixed number.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
This is just division-with-remainder from
[Lesson 6](../06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$17 \div 5 = 3$ remainder $2$. So $\dfrac{17}{5} = 3\tfrac{2}{5}$. The
quotient is the whole part; the remainder over the divisor is the
fractional part.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** What is $\dfrac{1}{2} \div \dfrac{1}{4}$? (How many quarters fit
in one half?)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Picture a half-circle. How many quarter-circles do you need to cover the
half-circle?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$\tfrac{1}{2} \div \tfrac{1}{4} = 2$** — two quarters fit in a half.

The general rule: dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by
its **reciprocal** (flip top and bottom):
$$\frac{a}{b} \div \frac{c}{d} = \frac{a}{b} \times \frac{d}{c}.$$

Here: $\tfrac{1}{2} \times \tfrac{4}{1} = \tfrac{4}{2} = 2$. ✓

This is one reason fraction division feels strange: dividing by a
*fraction less than 1* makes the result *bigger* than the original —
contradicting the intuition from whole-number division.
</details>

**S2.** A database stores user ages and computes "the average age". The
ages of three users are **22, 31, and 25**. What is the average? Is the
answer always a whole number?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Average = total ÷ count. The count is 3.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Total: $22 + 31 + 25 = 78$. Count: 3. Average: $78 \div 3 = 26$.
**26**.

But not always whole: if the ages had been 22, 31, 26, the total would
be 79, and $79 \div 3 = 26\tfrac{1}{3}$. Statistical computations almost
always produce non-whole numbers — the average is a *summary* of the
data, not necessarily a value that any individual user takes. We will
unpack the average properly in
[Lesson 14: Data and the Average](../14-data-and-average/index.qmd).
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"To add fractions, add numerators and add denominators."** This is
   wrong: $\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}$ would give $\tfrac{2}{4} =
   \tfrac{1}{2}$ — but the answer is *one whole*, not a half. Common
   denominators first; then add only the *top*.
2. **"A bigger denominator means a bigger fraction."** No — bigger
   denominator means *more pieces in the whole*, hence each piece is
   *smaller*. $\tfrac{1}{8}$ is smaller than $\tfrac{1}{2}$.
3. **"Dividing by a fraction always gives a smaller number."** As *S1*
   showed, the opposite happens when the divisor is less than 1.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [fraction](/glossary.qmd#fraction) $\tfrac{p}{q}$ is *the* answer
   to $p \div q$ — the leftover that wouldn't fit, written as a number
   in its own right.**
2. **The same fraction has many names ([equivalent
   fractions](/glossary.qmd#equivalent-fractions)) — multiplying top and
   bottom by the same nonzero number keeps the value unchanged.**
3. **To add or subtract, line the denominators up first; to multiply,
   multiply across; to divide, flip and multiply
   ([reciprocal](/glossary.qmd#reciprocal)).**

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you draw a picture showing why $\tfrac{2}{8}$ and $\tfrac{1}{4}$
   are the same number?
2. Without scrolling back, can you compute $\tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{1}{4}$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

- **3 yes:** ready for **[Lesson 8 — Decimals](../08-decimals/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** — fraction division is the most-mis-taught topic
  in primary maths.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P2 and P3*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](../06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)** —
  fractions are *exactly* the answer to division when the total doesn't
  split evenly. We invented them to fill the gap left by remainders.

### What this unlocks

- **[Decimals](../08-decimals/index.qmd)** — decimals are fractions
  whose denominators are powers of 10, written in place-value form.
- **[Ratio and Proportion](../09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)** —
  $\tfrac{p}{q}$ also expresses "the ratio $p$ to $q$".

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Fractions extend the natural numbers (Lesson
1) into the **rational numbers** $\mathbb{Q}$ — the second great number
system after $\mathbb{N}$. Tier 5 will revisit this construction
formally and meet the *irrationals* (numbers like $\sqrt{2}$ or $\pi$
that can't be written as fractions).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Probabilities are fractions: "70% chance" is
$\tfrac{7}{10}$. Loss functions are averages (fractions). Gradients are
ratios of small changes. Most of ML's numerical machinery lives in the
fraction world (and its decimal cousin).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** Aggregate queries (averages, percentages, ratios) all
produce fractional answers. SQL's `AVG()` is a fraction. Data
normalisation often divides each value by the maximum, producing values
in $[0, 1]$ — pure fractions.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Recipe scaling, currency conversion, slope of a
ramp, batting averages, taxi fares (per kilometre), interest rates —
fractions are how the world parameterises *rate* and *proportion*.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the pie-chart widget — a
  centuries-old fraction manipulative made interactive), Boole (the
  Wonder questions surface equivalence and improper fractions before
  naming), Russian school (P2 forces equivalence, P5 forces improper
  fractions), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse). Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*
  itself contains some of the most beautifully phrased fraction
  problems in mathematical history.
- **Self-study guarantees** all present.
:::
